This app gets a lot of attention as it tracks your progress, shows and gives you medals for your PR, and allows you to connect and share and get likes for accomplishments with other cyclist and runners.  I was suckered into paying for the full subscription by the hype this app gets with all the “if it isn’t logged on Strava it didn’t happen” acclaims.  Tonight I went on a near 4 hour ride and the app only tracked the duration and nothing else.  This app is overrated and glitchy to be useful.  

Map my ride is a much better app and you don’t need to pay extra to still get a great app experience.  Map my ride allows you to connect with other too but not as well as Strava.  However where it shines is if you want it to it gives you verbal updates in either variable time or distance increments with your current speed, distance, split pace, and average space.  Also what is cool is it will provide a starting timer so you don’t have to start the workout when you are stationary or just warming up.  

I actually did a quality test having both log a ride simultaneously and the stats were nearly the same however Strava gave me faster average speed so if you want to inflate your speed stats then Strava is for you.  

I now have to begrudgingly keep this app and make the best of it until my yearly subscription expires next January.  Don’t be a fool like me and give this app any of your money.   Keep it to humor your friends if you must.  Map my ride is so much better.

Why I put 3 stars: there are unnecessary issues with the app, notifications, and support. 
- no matter how may times you check that you don’t want notifications, you’re still inundated with them
- email notifications specifically, based on their help page, have been an issue for years and is still unresolved. if my settings have email notifications switched off, why am I still getting daily kudos and comment notifications in my email? 
- if you want to submit a support ticket, that landing page isn’t formatted for mobile (for a huge company, not checking your user experience on mobile is wild). the drop down doesn’t work to even start a help ticket on mobile, so you’ll never be able to submit one, which feels like an intentional “oversight” for a company running a internationally used app. 
- I understand that you can’t opt out of certain marketing and security emails, that’s not the problem. it’s the unnecessary emails that you can “opt out” of (you can’t, that option is fake). don’t provide that setting option if it’s not real — so then I can decide to delete my account and stop using the app. if the setting is there, make it actually work.

I downloaded Strava to “go on more walks.” What I got instead was a deeply intimate performance art platform that eroticizes productivity, surveillance, and self documentation in a way no dating app ever could.

There is nothing sexier than a glowing orange map of where I’ve been. Look at me. Witness me. I walked 4 miles and felt something. The pace? Slow. The elevation gain? Emotional. The time of day? Questionable. The commentary? Vulnerable enough to be inappropriate, but framed as “wellness.”

Strava knows when I’m active. It knows when I’m resting. It knows when I’m spiraling but calls it “recovery.” It rewards me with digital badges for consistency, which is frankly a kink. Every post feels like whispering into a public diary while pretending it’s just cardio.

Other apps ask, “What are you looking for Strava asks, “How did it feel?” And I answer. Every time.

For the most part, this is functional. I recently started using this instead of Nike Run Club in order to connect more with friends and family who exercise, and to be motivated by that. It is good for this. However, there are two large problems when compared to NRC.

(1) Free is stingy. With NRC you can always look up your best times (1 mile, 5K, 10K, etc.).   With Strava, for ***$80 per year*** you can see your best times. I mean, come on. I’m not asking for extremely detailed analysis of my exercise. This alone may have me going back to NRC at some point, or to record runs there after the fact.

(2) Glitchy auto-pause. In only 1 month using Strava, the auto-pause has randomly triggered twice for me and it remained on throughout the exercise until I saw it. This has happened with two family members and a friend in the past month, all who use different recording devices. Okay, so, I have removed autopause, but now need to remember to turn it off and on when I hit traffic signals.

Not the quality worth paying $80 for each year. Would I pay $10 one time to see my best times? Sure, I guess. But that’s not on the table.
